   Decision Making

   Fiscal Policy



     Professor Abbas Valadkhani

     Week 11











1




                          Fiscal Policy


    What is fiscal policy?

    ’é¦  Fiscal Policy      : Changes in federal taxes and purchases
       that are intended to achieve macroeconomic policy
       objectives, such as high employment, price stability and
       healthy rates of economic growth.

    ’é¦  Fiscal policy refers to the actions of the federal
       government, and not state, territory or local governments.








2




                                                                                                                      1

                          Fiscal policy

    Automatic stabilisers versus discretionary fiscal policy

    ’é¦  Automatic stabilisers          : Transfer payments and taxes that
       automatically increase or decrease along with the
       business cycle.

    ’é¦  Discretionary fiscal policy          : When the government is
       taking actions to change spending or taxes to achieve its
       economic objectives (fiscal policy).







3




                          Fiscal policy


    An overview of government spending and taxes

    ’é¦  Government expenditure includes government purchases
       plus all other federal government spending.

    ’é¦  Between 1970 and 2018 government expenditure
       fluctuated between approximately 18% and 36% as a
       proportion of GDP.

                                      Fiscal policy

      An overview of government spending and taxes, cont.

      ’é¦   The largest single area of government expenditure is
          social security and welfare payments.

      ’é¦   The largest single source of federal government revenue
          is from personal income taxation receipts, which is close to
          half of government revenue.

                                                                                                                                                                                                                              4

      Using Fiscal Policy to Influence
                       Aggregate Demand

     Expansionary fiscal policy

     ’é¦  Expansionary fiscal policy                  : Increases in government
        purchases and/or decreases in taxes in order to increase
        aggregate demand.

     ’é¦  An increase in government purchases will increase
        aggregate demand directly                 .

     ’é¦  A reduction in taxes has an                 indirect    effect on aggregate
        demand through the affect on disposable income.


9

10




                                                                                                                                       5

         Using fiscal policy to influence
                         aggregate demand

     Contractionary fiscal policy

     ’é¦  Contractionary fiscal policy                   : Decreases in government
        purchases and/or increases in taxes in order to reduce the
        increases in aggregate demand.
     ’é¦  A decrease in government purchases or an increase in
        taxes will reduce the rate of increase in aggregate demand,
        to reduce the inflation rate.
     ’é¦  Appropriate when the economy is above full employment
        equilibrium and the inflation rate is high.

    The Multiplier Effect and Government Purchases and
                                   Tax Multipliers

    The government purchases multiplier

    An increase in government purchases will increase
    aggregate demand by more than the initial amount
    of the increase in purchases.

    ’é¦   Autonomous expenditure: Expenditure that
        does not depend on the level of GDP.

    ’é¦   Induced expenditure: Expenditure that depends
        on the level of GDP. This expenditure is induced by
        the initial increase.


14




                                                                                                                         7

      The multiplier effect and government purchases and
                                  tax multipliers



    ’é¦  Multiplier effect: The process by which an
       increase in autonomous expenditure leads to a
       larger increase in real GDP.

    ’é¦  Multiplier: The increase in equilibrium real GDP
       divided by the increase in autonomous
       expenditure.






15








          A simple formula for the multiplier

   ’é¦   Marginal propensity to consume (MPC):                          the
       amount by which consumption changes when
       disposable income changes.


        Government purchases     multiplier ’ĆĮ change  in equilibrium  real GDP
                                            change   in government purchases





   One personŌĆÖs additional expenditure is another
   person's additional income.

         ŌĆóSecond person spends (consumes)                                          ŌĆ”800ŌĆ”....
         ŌĆóThird person receives $800, spends                                         ŌĆ”ŌĆ”640ŌĆ”.
              And so on ŌĆ”

     The tax multiplier

     ’é¦   Tax changes also have a multiplier effect.
     ’é¦   When taxes decrease income rises and so to does
         consumption.

             [*] ŌĆ£Balanced-budgetŌĆØ multiplier


     ŌĆóFrom the above example: a billion-dollar rise in                G,
        together with a billion-dollar rise in T, would increase
        GDP by one billion dollars.
     ŌĆóIn the simple Keynesian model, the ŌĆ£balanced-budgetŌĆØ
        multiplier is    one.
         ’āś  Perhaps a better name for this might be the
            ŌĆ£budget-neutralŌĆØ multiplier
         ’āś  In more complicated (and more realistic) models,
            this multiplier is less than one.
     ŌĆóKey insight:  In the short run          , a billion-dollar cut in T
        has a smaller     effect on output than a billion-dollar rise
     25 in G.

25




    How the federal budget can serve
             as an automatic stabiliser

    ’é¦  Federal government deficits increaseautomatically during
       economic contractions and recessions because:
         1.   Tax revenues fall
         2.   Unemployment benefit payments increase
    ’é¦  Federal government deficits decreaseautomatically during
       an economic expansion or boom because:
         1.   Tax revenues increase
         2.   Unemployment benefit payments decrease




26




                                                                                                            13

    Should the federal budget always
                           be balanced?


    ’é¦  When the economy is contracting, or in a recession, tax
       revenues fall and government spending rises, moving the
       budget into a deficit.
    ’é¦  When actual GDP is above its potential level during a
       boom, the budget automatically moves into a surplus ŌĆōtax
       revenues rise.
    ’é¦  To maintain a balanced budget every year could involve
       policies that might destabilise the economy.




27




    The Effects of Fiscal Policy in the
                               Long Run


    ’é¦  Supply-side policies       : Fiscal policies that have long-run
       effects by expanding the productive capacity of the
       economy and increasing the rate of economic growth.

    ’é¦  These policy actions primarily affect aggregate supply
       rather than aggregate demand, shifting the long-run
       aggregate supply curve to the right.







28




                                                                                                          14

   ’é¦  Lowering taxes can increase the incentive to work, and
      encourage investment.
   ’é¦  Reducing each of the following taxes can increase
      aggregate supply.

        1.   Personal income tax
        2.   Company income tax
        3.   Taxes on capital gains





29





    The Limits of Using Fiscal Policy
             to Stabilise the Economy



    There are two main problems associated with fiscal policy
    effectiveness:

    1.   Timing lags
    2.   Crowding out





30




                                                                                                          15

          Implementation difficulties:  time lags

       ŌĆó  Inside lags       :  time lags inside the policy-
          making system; due to
           ŌĆōcollection of statistical data
           ŌĆōanalysis to confirm what is happening
           ŌĆōdecision-making process  (e.g. Parliament)
           ŌĆōimplementation process
       ŌĆó  Outside lags        : between implementation and
          effects
       ŌĆóBecause of time lags, policy-makers and their
          advisers need to anticipate              future
     31   developments

31






              Other implementation difficulties


      ŌĆó   Uncertainty       about   key parameters       (e.g. the above
          ŌĆ£fractionŌĆØ, i.e. the     marginal propensity to
          consume     )

      ŌĆó   Trade-offs: expansionary fiscal policies may raise
          interest rates or increase inflation or taxation
          Example: how to finance an increase in G?
          First option:  raise T.  But this:-
            ŌĆōis politically unattractive
            ŌĆōwill partially offset the expansionary effects

     32

32




                                                                                                             16

          Borrow to finance an increase in G?


      A second option: govŌĆÖt can borrow from the
      public (e.g., selling bonds).  But in that case:-

         -upward pressure on interest rates,            reducing     private
             investment and consumer spending on durables

         -this ŌĆ£   crowding-outŌĆØ effect will partially offset the
             expansionary effects (see the next diagram)




    33

33






         Extra government spending on what?


      On what should the extra amount of G                   be
      spent?
      Sometimes this may not matter very much, as
      long as there is       some    extra spending.
      Examples:
          -A suggestion: one work gang digging up the
             road and another gang filling in.
          -A bridge essentially to nowhere.
          -A chequefor $900 to each taxpayer, or a
    34       shopping voucher for every registered voter
